instruction: What are the current best practices for deploying governed multi-agent AI systems with headless browser automation in production?
output: Production deployment of governed multi-agent AI systems with headless browser automation in 2026 follows a clear architectural pattern: goal-oriented agent teams operating within Zero Trust governance frameworks, using accessibility-tree-based browser interaction via Playwright MCP (not CSS selectors or screenshots), coordinated through persistent runtime gateways like OpenClaw with sub-agent spawning. Key practices include: (1) Multi-agent specialization - splitting browser work across navigation, reasoning, and verification agents; (2) Hybrid execution - AI handles ambiguous parts while deterministic scripts handle guaranteed-path steps; (3) Zero Trust security with RBAC, credential vaults where LLMs never see passwords, domain allowlists, and sandboxed execution; (4) Queue-based orchestration with Redis workers for parallel/sequential/hierarchical agent dispatch; (5) Human-in-the-loop escalation at critical decision points; (6) Full audit trails for compliance. The Playwright CLI reduces token usage 4x compared to MCP protocol. Docker hardening for headless browsers requires custom engineering for device auth and browser capabilities within strict container constraints. Sources: Microsoft Playwright MCP, n8n production guide, OpenClaw deployment at Context Studios, Browserless state of AI 2026, Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework.

rejected: To deploy multi-agent AI systems with browser automation, you should use Playwright or Puppeteer to control browsers, set up multiple agents to do different tasks, and make sure to add some security measures like authentication. You can use Docker to run the browsers and connect them to your AI system. Most teams use a central coordinator to manage the agents.
